title: Middle East Aluminum Supply Shock
catalyst: Iranian missile strikes on Emirates Global Aluminium's Al Taweelah complex and Aluminium Bahrain (Alba) — the world's largest single-site smelter — have removed nearly 3 million tonnes of annual smelting capacity, on top of the Hormuz blockade choking off alumina and bauxite logistics. Analysts have characterized it as 'probably the largest single supply shock a base metals market has suffered in the post-2000 era,' with a projected ~2 million tonne global deficit by year-end 2026.
thesis: Aluminum cannot be substituted in many of its end-uses (transmission lines, EV bodies, cans, aerospace), and global inventories were already thin entering the conflict. With Middle East capacity offline indefinitely and Chinese smelting capped by its 45 Mt policy ceiling, the marginal tonne must come from US, Canadian, or Norwegian producers — most of whom run at full utilization. Producers with operating US smelters captured under Section 232 tariffs are earning windfall margins on both LME-linked pricing and the Midwest premium, which has spiked to multi-year highs.

ticker_rationale.AA: Alcoa is the largest US primary aluminum producer with approximately 2.9 million tonnes of annual smelting capacity across the US, Canada, Norway, Iceland, and Australia — geographically insulated from Middle East risk. Every $100/tonne move in LME aluminum translates to roughly $290M in incremental EBITDA at full capacity, giving it the highest absolute dollar leverage to the price spike among publicly traded pure-plays.
ticker_rationale.CENX: Century Aluminum operates the only domestic smelters capable of producing high-purity aluminum for US defense applications (Hawesville, Sebree, Mt. Holly), making it a strategic supplier insulated from Section 232 tariff debates. While the stock has already rallied sharply on the Hormuz hedge thesis, the Midwest premium expansion and DoD priority allocation provide a continued earnings tailwind that consensus estimates have not fully absorbed.

risks: Ceasefire or rapid EGA/Alba reconstruction could normalize supply faster than expected, collapsing the Midwest premium
Demand destruction from elevated aluminum prices — beverage cans, autos, and construction substitute into steel or composites at sustained $3,500+/tonne levels
US smelters cannot ramp capacity meaningfully — they compete with AI data centers for grid power, so the upside is captured through price, not volume
CENX has already returned ~350% over the past year; entry timing matters and a profit-taking pullback is likely if any de-escalation headlines emerge
summary: Iranian strikes on EGA and Alba removed ~3 million tonnes of annual aluminum capacity, spiking LME prices to $3,571/tonne and creating windfall margins for geographically insulated US producers Alcoa and Century Aluminum.
body_md:
## Setup

The global aluminum market has suffered what analysts describe as the largest single supply shock a base metals market has experienced in the post-2000 era. On March 28, 2026, Iranian precision-guided munitions and loitering drones struck Emirates Global Aluminium's Al Taweelah smelting complex in Abu Dhabi (1.4 million tonnes/year capacity) and Aluminium Bahrain's (Alba) facility in Bahrain (1.65 million tonnes/year) [1][2]. EGA halted operations entirely; Alba was reduced to an estimated 30% utilization. Combined with earlier curtailments at Qatar's Qatalum smelter, nearly 3 million tonnes of annual smelting capacity — close to half of Middle East aluminum production — has been taken offline [1].

The situation escalated from a production crisis to a total logistical collapse on April 12, 2026, when the failure of peace negotiations in Islamabad prompted retaliatory moves that effectively closed the Strait of Hormuz to Western-aligned commercial traffic [3]. This second-order effect choked off alumina and bauxite shipments to surviving Middle East and Asian smelters, amplifying the direct production losses into a systemic supply chain failure.

## Why Now

Aluminum prices on the London Metal Exchange spiked to $3,571 per tonne by mid-April 2026 — levels not seen since the 2022 energy crisis [3]. But unlike the 2022 episode, which was driven by temporary European energy disruptions, this shock involves physical destruction of smelting infrastructure that takes 12-24 months to rebuild even under optimal conditions. Analysts project a ~2 million tonne global deficit by year-end 2026 [1].

Critically, the supply response is structurally constrained. China's smelting industry operates under a 45 million tonne policy ceiling, leaving no room for surge production. Russian aluminum faces sanctions-related logistical barriers. The marginal tonne of supply must come from US, Canadian, Norwegian, or Icelandic producers — most of whom already run at or near full utilization. US producers benefit additionally from Section 232 tariffs (50% on imported aluminum), which create a protected pricing environment on top of the global LME price increase [4].

## Company-Specific Edge

Alcoa (AA) is the largest US primary aluminum producer with approximately 2.9 million tonnes of annual smelting capacity geographically distributed across the US, Canada, Norway, Iceland, and Australia — entirely insulated from Middle East risk [5]. Net income surged from $60M ($0.26 EPS) in 2024 to $1.2 billion ($4.42 EPS) in 2025 on higher aluminum prices, and the current price environment is substantially stronger than the average realized price in 2025. Every $100/tonne move in LME aluminum translates to roughly $290M in incremental EBITDA at full capacity, giving Alcoa the highest absolute dollar leverage to the price spike among publicly traded pure-plays. At 10.2x forward P/E with D/E of 44.5%, the stock remains deeply discounted relative to its improved earnings power. The ELYSIS zero-carbon smelting JV with Rio Tinto provides long-term regulatory positioning [5].

Century Aluminum (CENX) operates the only domestic smelters capable of producing high-purity aluminum for US defense applications — Sebree and Mt. Holly (Hawesville was sold for AI data center redevelopment) [6]. The stock has delivered approximately 350% returns over the past year as investors adopted it as a "Hormuz hedge," but the earnings tailwind from elevated Midwest premiums and DoD priority allocation may not be fully reflected in consensus estimates. Century is accelerating the Mt. Holly restart to full capacity by June 2026, and the Oklahoma greenfield smelter JV with EGA (backed by a $500M DOE grant) positions the company for long-term domestic capacity growth — the first new US primary aluminum smelter since 1980 [6]. At 7.3x forward P/E, the valuation is extremely compressed on recovery earnings.

## Numbers That Matter

**Alcoa (AA)**:
- Revenue: $12.83B (FY2025), with 2026 production guidance of 2.4-2.6M metric tons
- Earnings: $4.42 EPS (FY2025), dramatically improved from $0.26 in FY2024
- Valuation: 10.2x forward P/E, D/E 44.5%, ROE 19.7%
- LME leverage: ~$290M incremental EBITDA per $100/tonne price increase at full capacity
- Liquidity: $443M daily traded value

**Century Aluminum (CENX)**:
- Valuation: 7.3x forward P/E, PEG ~0.5, D/E 61.6%
- Q4 2025 adjusted EBITDA: $171M (+$70M sequentially)
- Q1 2026 guidance: $215-235M adjusted EBITDA
- Strategic: Only US producer of defense-grade high-purity aluminum
- Catalyst: Oklahoma greenfield smelter — first new US smelter since 1980

## Bear Case

The most immediate risk is a ceasefire or rapid diplomatic resolution. Any de-escalation headlines could trigger a sharp reversal in aluminum prices and a profit-taking selloff in both stocks — CENX in particular, given its 350% run, would be vulnerable to a 30-50% pullback on ceasefire news [4]. Rapid EGA/Alba reconstruction (unlikely but possible with sovereign wealth fund backing) could normalize supply within 12 months rather than the expected 18-24 months.

Demand destruction is a real concern at sustained $3,500+/tonne levels. Beverage can producers, automotive OEMs, and construction companies are already substituting steel and composite alternatives where technically feasible. If prices remain elevated for multiple quarters, structural demand destruction could partially offset the supply shortfall.

US smelters face a fundamental capacity constraint: they cannot meaningfully ramp production because they compete with AI data centers for grid power allocation. The upside for AA and CENX is captured through price, not volume — meaning a price reversal would eliminate the thesis without any operational deterioration.

Alcoa carries environmental liabilities including 101 water pollution violations at the Warrick County smelter and multiple Superfund cleanup settlements [5]. Century's culture scores (Glassdoor 2.2/5 on infrastructure report) and pure commodity exposure add operational risk.

## Conclusion

The aluminum supply shock thesis is a time-limited, event-driven opportunity anchored in physical supply destruction that cannot be quickly rebuilt. The ~3 million tonnes of offline Middle East capacity, combined with the Hormuz logistics blockade and China's 45 Mt smelting ceiling, creates a structural deficit that benefits geographically insulated Western producers. Alcoa offers the highest absolute dollar leverage at a deeply discounted valuation (10.2x forward P/E), while Century Aluminum provides concentrated US defense-grade exposure at 7.3x forward P/E. The key monitoring points are ceasefire negotiations, EGA/Alba reconstruction timelines, and LME aluminum prices — any sustained move below $3,000/tonne would signal thesis weakening.
invalidation: A formal US-Iran ceasefire is signed and EGA/Alba announce reconstruction timelines of less than 6 months, collapsing the Midwest premium below $0.15/lb
LME aluminum prices fall below $2,800/tonne for two consecutive weeks, signaling demand destruction is outpacing supply tightening
China lifts its 45 Mt smelting cap or announces emergency capacity additions exceeding 1 million tonnes, introducing new marginal supply
